title: Proyecto Ouroboros — Meta-Compiler DAG
description: Asymmetric Mixture-of-Models pipeline: local Qwen decomposes a raw requirement into atomic sub-tasks, three concurrent DeepInfra specialists (Llama-3.1-70B Architect, Qwen-2.5-Coder-32B Engineer, Mixtral-8x22B Red Team) design, code and audit a software patch in parallel, and the local Qwen merges all outputs via hmem into an executable .patch + Pytest suite.

decomposer.role: Requirements analyst. Reads the raw technical requirement and decomposes it into three atomic sub-tasks: (1) data structure design, (2) implementation, (3) concurrency audit. Writes the decomposition manifest to hmem.

architect.role: Stateless mathematical architect. Takes sub-task #1 from hmem and produces the formal specification of the Merkle DAG data structure: node schema, hash function, insertion algorithm, traversal guarantees, and complexity bounds. Output is strictly specification, no implementation code.

engineer.role: Stateless code specialist. Takes the Architect's mathematical spec from hmem and sub-task #2. Writes the Python implementation: MerkleNode dataclass, MerkleDAG class with insert/lookup/audit methods, thread-safe locking strategy using threading.RLock, and integration shim for the existing MemoryCatalog interface.

red-team.role: Stateless adversarial concurrency auditor. Does NOT write code. Reads the Engineer's implementation from hmem and sub-task #3. Identifies race conditions, deadlock vectors, livelock scenarios, and Inference-DoS attack surfaces. If critical vulnerabilities are found, outputs a structured threat report with severity labels. CRITICAL findings trigger a Tombstone fence in hmem.

merger.role: Local patch packager. Restores its .hlx session from the decomposition phase. Reads the final validated implementation from hmem (post-tombstone resolution) and packages everything into a unified diff .patch file and a Pytest test suite.

dag.concurrency_note: Architect runs first. Engineer starts once Architect completes (reads spec from hmem). Red Team runs concurrently with Engineer from Engineer's start, auditing intent from the spec; performs second-pass audit on Engineer's code once written to hmem.

tasks.prompt: You are a requirements analyst for a systems software project. Read the technical requirement below and decompose it into exactly three atomic sub-tasks: (1) DATA STRUCTURE DESIGN — what must be specified mathematically, (2) IMPLEMENTATION — what code must be written, (3) CONCURRENCY AUDIT — what concurrent failure modes must be validated. Output a structured manifest with the three sub-tasks numbered and labelled. Be precise and technical.

You are a mathematical systems architect. Your sub-task is DATA STRUCTURE DESIGN. Using the decomposition manifest from context, produce the formal specification of a Merkle DAG in-memory structure to replace SQLite FTS5 as the audit backend. Include: node schema (fields, types), hash function selection and rationale, insertion algorithm (pseudocode), traversal and lookup complexity (O-notation), and thread-safety guarantees at the structural level. Output is SPECIFICATION ONLY — no Python or Rust code.

You are a code specialist. Your sub-task is IMPLEMENTATION. Using the Architect's formal specification from context, write a Python implementation with: (1) a MerkleNode dataclass with content, hash, parent_hash, and timestamp fields, (2) a MerkleDAG class with insert(content), lookup(hash), audit_chain(root_hash) and to_dict() methods, (3) thread-safe access via threading.RLock for all write operations, (4) a MemoryCatalogShim class that wraps MerkleDAG to match the existing MemoryCatalog.observe() and MemoryCatalog.search() interface signatures. Output clean, runnable Python code only.

You are an adversarial concurrency auditor. Your sub-task is CONCURRENCY AUDIT. Do NOT write replacement code. Read the Engineer's implementation from context and identify: (1) any race conditions in the RLock usage patterns, (2) potential deadlock vectors if MerkleDAG methods are called re-entrantly during callback chains, (3) any livelock or starvation scenarios under high ThreadPool concurrency, (4) Inference-DoS attack surfaces (e.g. unbounded recursion in audit_chain). For each finding state: SEVERITY (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), LOCATION (method name), and DESCRIPTION. If you find CRITICAL or HIGH severity issues, start your response with 'TOMBSTONE:' followed by the threat summary.

You are a patch packager. Restore your session from the decomposition phase. Read the validated implementation and the Red Team audit from context. Generate: (1) a unified diff in standard .patch format showing the migration from the old MemoryCatalog SQLite backend to the new MerkleDAG backend, (2) three Pytest test functions: test_insert_and_lookup(), test_audit_chain_integrity(), test_concurrent_writes_no_deadlock(). Format the output as two clearly labelled sections: PATCH: and TESTS:
